name: Iris — Higher-Order Concurrent Separation Logic
summary: Jung, Krebbers et al.'s Iris is a language-generic higher-order concurrent separation logic framework in Coq/Rocq, providing a single foundation for many program logics. Won the 2023 Alonzo Church Award. Higher-order ghost state with user-definable resource algebras unifies invariants, atomicity, and partial-commutative-monoid reasoning. Foundation for RustBelt, RefinedRust, RefinedC, and 50+ research projects.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Jung, Krebbers et al.'s **Iris** is a language-generic higher-order concurrent separation logic framework in Coq/Rocq, providing a single foundation for many program logics. Won the 2023 Alonzo Church Award. Higher-order ghost state with user-definable resource algebras unifies invariants, atomicity, and partial-commutative-monoid reasoning. **Iris Proof Mode (IPM/MoSeL)** Coq tactics make embedded separation-logic proofs as ergonomic as native Coq proofs. "Iris-in-Iris" self-hosting: Iris's own model is built within itself.

Foundation for RustBelt, RefinedRust, RefinedC, many other verifications; used in 50+ research projects. The closest thing the field has to a universal substrate for separation-logic verification.

sections.title: Higher-order ghost state and resource algebras
sections.content:
Iris's central technical idea is **user-definable resource algebras**: a partial commutative monoid the verifier instantiates per-proof, used as the algebraic substrate for ghost state. Invariants, atomicity tokens, lock ownership, and lifetime tokens are all encoded as ghost-state constructions over chosen resource algebras rather than as logic primitives. A proof developer adds a new ghost-state mechanism by defining a resource algebra rather than extending the logic.

The logic is **step-indexed** — a stratification by approximation depth that lets propositions reason about higher-order state (functions stored in the heap, recursive predicates) without paradox. Combined with the higher-order assertion language, this admits assertions like "every value satisfying predicate P implies the value behaves linearly" — predicates over predicates that ordinary first-order separation logic cannot express.

sections.title: Iris Proof Mode and tooling
sections.content:
**Iris Proof Mode (IPM/MoSeL)** is a Coq tactic library that embeds separation-logic proof contexts inside Coq proof contexts. Spatial hypotheses (those consuming linear resources) are tracked separately from persistent ones; tactics like `iIntros`, `iApply`, `iSplit` mirror native Coq tactics but operate on the embedded logic. The result is that an Iris proof feels like a Coq proof — the same edit-rebuild loop, the same proof scripts, the same tooling — even though it discharges separation-logic obligations.

MoSeL generalises IPM beyond Iris to other modal separation logics, so the same tactic infrastructure underlies a family of verification efforts.

sections.title: RustBelt, RefinedRust, and the Iris ecosystem
sections.content:
Iris is the substrate of **RustBelt** (Jung et al., POPL 2018), which mechanises Rust's soundness; the **lifetime logic** that captures Rust's borrow checker is an Iris-derived logic with borrow propositions. **RefinedRust** (Gaeher et al., PLDI 2024) extends the same Iris model with refinement predicates and a more realistic operational semantics (Radium); **RefinedC** uses Iris for foundational verification of annotated C; **Tree Borrows** (Villani et al., PLDI 2025) formalises Rust's aliasing model on Iris substrate.

Status (as of 2026-04): Iris is the de facto foundation for new separation-logic research projects, with 50+ published verifications using its substrate. Its Coq mechanisation is large but stable enough that downstream projects share lemma libraries, tactic infrastructure, and proof patterns.
